sentences.text: Yeah.
Yeah.
My name is Nathan Fefer.
F-E-F-E-R.
Yes.
Now, I'm 82.
In Poland.
In a small village, Orzenay.
O-R-Z-E-N-A-Y.
Near Kovel.
A big city.
K-O-V-E-L.
Yeah, I have six sisters and one brother.
Yeah.
Pasi, Lea, Sarah, Zelda, Libby, and Rachael.
My brother's name was Mandel.
Melach.
Fega.
We had a store, a grocery store.
And we had a couple acres land with a barn.
And we raise a couple cows.
We really made a nice living there.
The town was there about 400 Jewish families.
And we had a rabbi.
We had two synagogues.
And we have a Yeshiva there and a [INAUDIBLE].
It a-- it was nice, you know.
We live between Ukrainian people.
One side was Ukrainian.
And we were in the middle.
And on the other side was a Polish village.
So the Ukrainian people never bothered with us.
I used to go 10:00, 12:00 in the night.
They never touch us.
And we make business with the Ukrainian people.
That's it.
Did you grow up in a religious home?
Yeah.
Yeah.
I keep Shabbas, everything kosher.
I keep kosher now here, too.
There-- there in Europe?
Yeah, we make a nice living, you know.
And we all go to school, you know.
We make a nice living.
Not millionaires, but very good off.
You know, we made a nice living.
Public school.
Well, I think not bad.
Well, I like-- I-- I was always, you know with the Zionist organization.
And later then, I grow up a little older, I was more on the left side.
It was [INAUDIBLE].
Oh, we used to go out, you know, dance, you know.
It was organizations there.
It was a library, a little library.
It was OK.
Very good.
I was raised like-- like-- like a king.
[LAUGHS]
Well, you know.
He was a religious man, but not fanatic.
So when I want to do something else, he didn't bother me, see?
Like, he-- he didn't see it, you know?
It was OK.
I was the king.
[LAUGHS]
She was a nice, quite woman.
She was busy to raise eight kids, you know?
She was a very nice, quiet woman.
Oh, yeah.
Chickens.
There's big challahs.
I was a bad eater, you know?
A very bad eater, even now.
So I don't like to eat lox.
She used to make special for me matzoh.
Just for me to make matzoh.
I think we were about a year, or two years from each other.
Oh, yeah.
Five got married.
Yeah.
Two, three were in the same village.
Well, was-- always in good-- in good friends, you know?
We-- we didn't fight.
To Yeshiva.
Because my father wants me, you know, to learn Talmud and everything, you know.
So half a day, it was with Yeshiva.
And half of a day, we had private two teachers to teach us Hebrew and Polish.
It was good.
No.
I can't say no.
Maybe in the bigger cities.
But the [INAUDIBLE], the Ukrainian people, they used to fight with the Polish people.
They left us alone.
And if something happened, you know, we were fighters.
We fight again, you know?
Like, it was [INAUDIBLE].
And if some you know, Ukrainian guy was strong, we liked to make a little bit trouble.
So we give him back.
So he quiet down, you know.
We were fighting good.
But it was very good.
You know, not too bad, you know?
When you look back on those early days of being a young boy, what stands out the most?
Well, it was good.
I was a strong man, you know.
I was always in the business, you know.
I was the business man from the family.
And I enjoyed this, you know.
Yeah.
But I don't see-- I didn't see, because they died before, you know.
No.
One, I remember, like-- like you know, through a sleep.
But I can't even recognize, you know.
Oh, yeah, many.
I lost 70 people from my family.
Oh, yeah.
You know, like weddings, you know, together, like, in the synagogue, you know.
Yeah, we were in good times.
No.
In Europe, it was not like this.
Everybody separate, you know.
Oh, Passover was a-- was a holiday, you know.
They used to make wine.
My father bought grapes.
You know, and lady squeezes this in a bottle.
And it has to stay two weeks, you know.
[INAUDIBLE] wine, matzohs, you know.
It was a nice, nice Passover.
Yeah, later in the end it was already machines that they make matzohs.
But we bake by hand, you know.
Cause you know, if you bake by hand, it's more kosher, like they say, you know.
My sisters, the father, the mother.
We had a big family, you know.
Oh, yeah.
Oh, yeah, Passover, [INAUDIBLE], Rosh Hashanah, [INAUDIBLE].
You know, [INAUDIBLE] they used to get drunk.
They used the dance in the streets with the Torahs.
And if somebody got married, they make the hoop and [INAUDIBLE].
We were not afraid.
Nobody touched us.
Oh, yeah.
Oh, they go around.
They talk.
They walk.
Sometimes a kiss.
[LAUGHS]
Oh, they're starting to change when Hitler came to power, you know.
We felt already a little bit, but we didn't know.
We know nothing.
We felt, because there was rumors.
Hitler do this.
Hitler does this in Germany.
But we didn't believe it.
Because-- because before the First War, the Germans were good people, you know?
So we didn't believe it.
No, until the war started, until they occupied Poland, you know.
When the war broke out, he occupied Poland.
So he made the deal with-- with Stalin-- two gangsters, you know.
He took half Poland.
And Hitler took half Poland.
So we were on the half Poland with the Russian.
And half Poland was with the Germans, you know.
So with the Russians, they didn't bother us, you know.
Did your life change at all with the Russians?
Well, yeah.
You could make business, you know.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] If you rich, they took away everything, you know.
But still, the life, it didn't bother you though.
The life was safe, you know.
You have to go according their law.
That's it.
[INAUDIBLE] didn't like it, but no choice, you know.
Well, first of all, no business.
If you have a store and merchandise, they took away.
And they-- and they sold it, you know.
It was another life already.
But still, you can go to the synagogue, you know.
But it was not so free like before, you know.
But the life was secured, you know.
Yeah, everybody has the money [INAUDIBLE] the penny.
But you've got, you know, merchandise.
You sold this, they give you something else, you know.
You tried to make a living, you know.
And I worked for them, you know.
I was manager in a store.
And they got a store every farmer used to-- to bring the product there, you know, like, potatoes, feed, this and this.
And I have-- and later, they came with trucks.
And they took away, you know.
So I was working there.
Nothing.
Nothing.
There [INAUDIBLE] sold out.
That's it.
There was no more store.
It was no private there.
Everything went to the government.
Until the war broke out. '41.
'41 On Sunday-- the first day is Sunday.
There was [? mobilization ?] in the Russian army.
Well, they took us-- they took us into the army.
And I was-- I never had a rifle in my life.
And they gave me a rifle, you know.
And we were fighting the Germans.
Well, it was a public, in the-- in the-- in the city.
This you have to go to the police.
And that's it.
They took you away, you know.
They told me I have to go to register with the army.
And that's it.
They took me away and put a Russian [INAUDIBLE] with a rifle, with grenades, you know.
Yeah, they had the Russian uniforms, the blouse, you know, with the boots you know.
Yeah.
We were, but later we disappeared.
Everybody has his own job, you know.
But I never saw them again, you know.
They took me to the army.
There was a-- to the army, and then with the army, and then on the front, you know.
It was 50 miles from my town.
It was right on the border with the Germans, you know.
They were fighting with the Germans not-- not to long, you know.
Yeah, I know from my [INAUDIBLE].
But later we-- and a matter of fact, we [INAUDIBLE] we didn't recognize each other.
[LAUGHS] They took the hair off with everything.
We didn't recognize each other.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah.
The rifle.
And there was a-- and then I went with them, you know.
I remember like, in the night time, they-- they give me a long roll of the telephone wires.
And one Russian soldier went with me.
And we go near the Germans.
And then later when [INAUDIBLE], the leader started shooting.
So I was deaf.
So he talked to me, I told him I didn't hear nothing.
He said, he put in the ears the finger, then you wouldn't hear nothing.
So then after two days fighting, the Germans advance.
And we-- and we go back, back, back, back.
[INAUDIBLE] because the Ukrainian, didn't like the-- the Russian people, the communists.
They were against the Russian people.
So they have a chance to run away.
They left everything on the road.
And they run away.
So why did the Germans advance?
Because the Russians fall apart, you know.
Until later, they were-- later in Stalingrad, they picked up.
But after two months, they took me in prisoners of war, you know, with the Ukrainian people.
Where were you when they took you?
It was, you know, near Kiev, you know.
Yeah.
But if they collapsed the army, they took everybody.
They took me.
Took me away.
And they put me in jail.
No, no, no.
It was the Russian.
No, no.
They don't say nothing.
In the [? war ?] time, you know-- see, Stalin was very bad.
But when the war broke out for the Jews, he open.
He says everybody wants to run away to Russia can go away without no papers.
Nobody let in every Jew to the country.
He did it.
He killed later, but he did it.
But the Jews did have the same right as everybody in Russia.
Thousands and thousands and thousands of Jews saved, because he let them the Jews into Russia.
Because, you know, they-- they took around maybe 500, 600 guys.
And you have to put your hands up, and that's it.
No choice.
Yeah, it was non-Jews there.
They took me to a prison-- to a-- a prisoner of war, to a jail, in Kovel, a big jail.
Then after two weeks, they took us out by trucks and put us to the Chelm concentration camp.
The same.
They didn't bother, no.
Everybody was the same.
See the Germans, if they didn't have to do it, they wouldn't do it, you know.
Oh, yeah.
Yeah.
Well, it was in a good condition.
You know, physically good condition.
Oh, the prison.
It looks like a prison, you know.
It was a small rooms.
Everybody has his own room.
When they took me to the army, they were crying.
So they dead, and I'm alive.
Sure.
They were crying, you know.
That's it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then they took me to Chelm camp.
They-- they took people by trucks, you know.
Not just me, but Ukrainian people, too.
Everybody, you know.
Jewish, and non-Jewish, Ukrainian people, and Jewish, too.
No.
No.
No.
It doesn't take too long, probably an hour and a half or two hours.
It's not too far, you know.
Didn't know where I was going.
I didn't know.
I got there.
And they put us in the barracks, you know.
It was just a-- [INAUDIBLE] just starting to build a camp.
It was there, a camp, a death camp, no food, no nothing, you know.
[INAUDIBLE].
And they took us to work, you know.
Because every-- every day was 20, 30 dead people from there, from hunger, from death.
So they took the Jewish people.
We have to take them there to the graves.
So from our camp there was about 5 miles, a little wooden area.
They dig a big, a big hole with bulldozers.
And we used to-- and they made a box with the 2 by 4 on one side, and a 2 by 4 on the other side.
And four people was carrying the box with the dead person.
Till we reach there, a lot of people, you know, died.
Of course, sometimes it was a rainy day.
With mud, you couldn't do it.
So the guy took a gun and-- and killed him.
So till they reached there, was already two three people in one box.
This was the worst job in your life, you know.
When you first got to this camp and you got off the truck, can you describe exactly what happened to you?
They took us off.
They put the Ukrainian people separate, the Jewish people separate, the Polish people separate.
That's it.
Oh, right away they separated everybody, separated everybody.
They put me in a barrack there with the Jewish people.
It was just-- just to sleep on the floor, was nothing there.
Even the roof was not finished there.
Was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in November, I think was it.
No, it was cold, rain, everything.
[INAUDIBLE] the blouse from the army just.
I was wearing two months, the same blouse.
Shoes.
If you have a good pair of shoes, they took away from you.
They give you their shoes, you know.
Wooden shoes just to survive.
They were from Poland, you know.
They was from Russia, too, you know.
But the most Russian of the prisoners, the Jews, they killed [INAUDIBLE].
Yeah.
A little bit soup, water.
In the morning, they used to give you like tea, you know, but nothing.
And then in the day time, later, for supper they give you a piece of bread with a little bit soup, watery soup.
And that's it, you know.
They woke in the morning.
Sure, they [INAUDIBLE] work in the morning.
They came-- they came here two soldiers.
They-- they took you.
And you have to go with them to work.
And one time, we worked there outside the camp, not too far maybe, across the road.
So in the meantime, the Hitler Youth came to this camp, to the Jewish camp.
And they start beating the Jews.
The Germans [INAUDIBLE] with us, we were about 10 people working there.
He was a very nice, good German.
He saw what was going on.
But 4 o'clock-- it's 4 o'clock, you have to finish the work.
But the Germans, a law's a law.
It's a law to [INAUDIBLE].
And it's a law to this.
So he saw what's going on.
When he will take us there, we'll get beaten up.
But he has no choice.
So he told us, clean, clean, clean up good.
Clean up the [INAUDIBLE].
Clean up good.
Just to take a couple more minutes until this go over.
He was a very good, a good German, you know.
Do you remember his name?
No.
No.
But he knew, he was for sure, you know, we were going to be beaten up there.
So he kept us a little bit more, more just to save us.
[INAUDIBLE] a lot of the people was injured, crying.
So what are you going to do?
Crying.
Crying to God.
Well, you know, sometimes yes and sometimes no.
If you see they take a rabbi with a tallis and tefilllin, and they kill them, so-- so sometimes you think is no God.
And sometimes, yes.
And most, I believe in my father.
When I have bad times, you know, in sleep when I-- when I saw my father, I knew right away I'll be saved.
Not-- not in Chelm.
But in another camp, I made-- I made friends.
I was about probably 2-- 23 or 24, probably.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
The age where you go in the army, you know.
About two and a half months, you know.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And then later came a German.
He said he wants 100 people, work people, shoemakers, tailors, [INAUDIBLE], carpenters.
So I said I'm a carpenter.
So what are you going to lose?
You're going to live there another month.
And you dead anyways.
So [INAUDIBLE].
During the time that you were there, had other-- had other transports also come in?
New transports?
Sure.
Sure.
My [INAUDIBLE] already 39,000.
I don't know.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Same camp, everybody lives for himself, you know, just to survive, you know.
Oh, sure.
Every morning.
God forbid one runs away.
So he took a line with people.
Then one SS man with a stick, and he count 1, 2, 3, like this, with a stick.
They were searching around, you know.
If they find them, they hung him up.
They hung them up.
And everybody has to go out and see he's hung.
And the Germans stood with a [INAUDIBLE] and you pick things, so then you can turn around the man who hangs on the rope.
If you have to look right, God forbid you don't look.
You have to look right at the person.
Yeah.
You mean dead people?
Who knows.
They count if you [INAUDIBLE].
Oh, yeah.
Oh, sure, sure.
When I was one day passing the camp to work, I saw one guy.
There was a telephone post.
He was tied up to the telephone post with ropes and a big carrot in his mouth.
And the sun was hot.
And it was like this.
Probably, he died there.
I don't know.
I thought he was Jewish, or not Jewish.
This I don't know.
But I saw him there.
That's what they did, you know.
